name: Dash
description: A short committed burst of movement in a chosen direction.
design_notes: Dash works best when the player can predict its distance, commitment, and recovery. Decide whether it is a traversal verb, an evasive combat tool, or a limited escape option before tuning recharge rules. Collision handling is central: a dash that clips through walls or loses direction on slopes will feel unreliable even when the numbers look reasonable.
design_purpose: Let the player rapidly reposition without replacing ordinary walking and jumping.
Create skill expression through direction choice, spacing, and recovery timing.
Support encounter and level designs that ask for quick commitment under pressure.
player_fantasy: The player feels agile and decisive, able to slip through danger or cross a gap at the last possible moment.

parameters.name: dash_distance
parameters.description: Total displacement target before collision truncation.

parameters.name: dash_travel_ms
parameters.description: Number of milliseconds used to cover the dash displacement.

parameters.name: dash_recharge_seconds
parameters.description: Delay before another dash charge becomes available.

parameters.name: dash_input_buffer_ms
parameters.description: Input buffer accepted before the dash becomes legal.

parameters.name: dash_invulnerability_frames
parameters.description: Frames that ignore eligible incoming damage during the burst.

edge_cases: Dashing into a concave corner should stop or slide consistently without tunneling through thin collision.
A buffered dash pressed before landing must choose whether to use ground rules, air rules, or be rejected.
Dash direction should remain stable if the character flips, target lock changes, or input device changes mid-burst.
Knockback, stun, teleport, or cutscene control must cancel dash state without leaving recharge or invulnerability stuck.
common_bugs: Dash moves by transform position and bypasses collision sweeps, causing wall clips at low frame rates.
Recharge starts on button press instead of successful activation, rewarding invalid repeated inputs.
Invulnerability frames outlast the visual dash because damage immunity is cleared by animation events.
Slope projection changes dash distance enough that uphill and downhill dashes feel like different mechanics.
balancing_notes: Short recharge and long invulnerability turn dash into a primary defense; long recharge makes it a tactical escape.
If dash cancels attacks, reloads, or hit stun, it becomes part of the combat economy and should be costed there.
Levels built around dash should expose readable landing space; hidden collision makes failures feel arbitrary.
accessibility_notes: Support input buffering and consider a separate toggle for double-tap dash if repeated rapid taps are required.
Pair readiness with visual, audio, and optional haptic cues so players are not forced to track a tiny UI element.

implementation_notes.unity: Use an explicit movement-state enum and feed the final desired velocity into CharacterController.Move or Rigidbody movement from FixedUpdate; keep dash and jump assists outside animation callbacks. For Dash, validate this path with parameters such as dash_distance and dash_travel_ms.
implementation_notes.godot: Keep the mechanic state on CharacterBody2D or CharacterBody3D, update velocity in _physics_process, and separate input buffering from collision response. For Dash, validate this path with parameters such as dash_distance and dash_travel_ms.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use CharacterMovement with a custom movement state or Gameplay Ability; gate activation through prediction-safe input and reconcile movement corrections cleanly in networked play. For Dash, validate this path with parameters such as dash_distance and dash_travel_ms.
implementation_notes.web: Represent the mechanic as deterministic state in the simulation loop, then apply resolved displacement before rendering through Canvas, Phaser, Three.js, or a custom engine. For Dash, validate this path with parameters such as dash_distance and dash_travel_ms.

example_games.title: Celeste
example_games.note: Uses dash as a core precision-platforming action.

example_games.title: Hades
example_games.note: Uses dash for combat evasion and repositioning.
